claims.evidence_text: Oakes et al. (2016) concluded that it takes approximately 300-400 growing degree days (GDD) for the plant to produce three leaves and form its first tiller... a grower's goal should be to plant on a date that gives them the best opportunity to accumulate 300-400 GDD as soon as possible
claims.claim_summary: Wheat needs about 300-400 GDD to produce three leaves and form its first tiller.

claims.evidence_text: The ideal planting date is estimated to be the date of the first annual fall freeze plus or minus one week (Thomason et al. 2019).
claims.claim_summary: Ideal winter wheat planting date is the first annual fall freeze date plus or minus one week.

claims.evidence_text: Tillering begins in the fall and then enters a dormant period as temperatures reach below freezing (32 F). From February to early March, once temperatures consistently reach the mid-30s and 40s, dormancy will break and tillering will continue
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat tillering begins in fall, pauses below freezing (32 F), and resumes Feb-early March when temperatures reach mid-30s to 40s F.

claims.evidence_text: If tiller density is less than 50 tillers per square foot, a nitrogen application is recommended at GS 25 and at GS 30... if the crop has fewer than 50 tillers, an application of 40-60 pounds of nitrogen per acre is needed to stimulate further tiller development
claims.claim_summary: Tiller density of 50 tillers/ft2 is the threshold for whether spring N is needed at GS25 in winter wheat.

claims.evidence_text: If pre-plant fertilizer is needed, only a moderate amount of nitrogen (15-30 lbs./acre) is necessary to promote tillering and reduce the possibility of nitrogen loss by leaching during dormancy
claims.claim_summary: Pre-plant fall N of 15-30 lb/acre promotes wheat tillering while limiting leaching loss.

claims.evidence_text: if the crop has fewer than 50 tillers, an application of 40-60 pounds of nitrogen per acre is needed to stimulate further tiller development
claims.claim_summary: If wheat has fewer than 50 tillers/ft2, apply 40-60 lb N/acre to stimulate tillering.

claims.evidence_text: A pre-plant fertilizer in the fall is recommended to promote emergence, initial tillering, and root growth... Nitrogen applications should be taken into consideration again once the crop breaks dormancy and resumes active growth. In Virginia, this occurs around late February and is categorized as growth stage 25.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat N timing: fall pre-plant for tillering/roots, then spring application at GS25 (dormancy break) and GS30 based on tiller density.

claims.evidence_text: This is especially true if soil nitrate levels are below 10 parts per million (ppm) NO3 -N in the top 6 inches of the soil profile. However, this is not recommended if nitrate levels are at or above 30 ppm NO3 -N
claims.claim_summary: Pre-plant N recommended for wheat when soil nitrate is below 10 ppm NO3-N (top 6 in); not recommended at or above 30 ppm.

claims.evidence_text: Nitrogen (N) along with sufficient levels of sulfur (S) are essential nutrients for the production of winter wheat... Because S is an important component of all proteins, it is required together with N for protein synthesis
claims.claim_summary: Sulfur is an essential nutrient for winter wheat, required with N for protein synthesis.

claims.evidence_text: compared to any other growth stage, the greatest amount of daily potassium (K) uptake occurs between the beginning and end of tillering because it is needed to maintain cell turgidity and nutrient mobility in the plant
claims.claim_summary: Wheat potassium uptake peaks during tillering, maintaining cell turgidity and nutrient mobility.

claims.evidence_text: Phosphorous (P) is also crucial during tiller development because it is known to affect the amount and survival of wheat tillers
claims.claim_summary: Phosphorus is crucial during wheat tiller development, affecting tiller number and survival.
